title: Huntington's Disease
chief_complaint: My wife says I've been irritable, moody, and my personality has changed. I've been having involuntary jerky movements of my arms and face. I can't control them. My father had 'some kind of neurological disease' and died in a nursing home at 55.

physical_exam.Neurologic: Choreiform movements: Brief, irregular, non-stereotyped movements of face, neck, and upper extremities. Writhing movements of fingers (piano-playing movements). Motor impersistence: Unable to maintain tongue protrusion ('jack-in-the-box tongue') or grip ('milkmaid grasp'). Gait: Dancing, lurching gait.

hidden_data.mri_brain: Bilateral caudate nucleus atrophy with resultant ex vacuo dilatation of frontal horns (boxcar ventricles). Putamen atrophy.
hidden_data.genetic: HTT gene: CAG repeat expansion 47 repeats (normal <26, reduced penetrance 36-39, full penetrance >40). Confirms Huntington's disease.

correct_diagnosis: Huntington's Disease (CAG Repeat 47 - Full Penetrance) - Stage 2
key_learning_points: HD = autosomal dominant CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in HTT gene on chromosome 4. Anticipation: Earlier onset in successive generations (especially paternal transmission)
Triad: Chorea (involuntary movements), Cognitive decline (executive dysfunction → dementia), Psychiatric (depression, irritability, psychosis, suicide risk)
MRI: Caudate atrophy → boxcar ventricles (pathognomonic). PET: Reduced glucose metabolism in caudate
Treatment: Tetrabenazine/Deutetrabenazine (VMAT2 inhibitors) for chorea. Antipsychotics (Olanzapine, Risperidone) for chorea + psychosis. SSRIs for depression. NO disease-modifying therapy exists
Genetic counseling essential. Each child has 50% risk. Pre-symptomatic testing available with psychological support. Juvenile HD if onset <20 years (CAG >60)
